turns.text: Hello Darlene, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It feels... heavy, I guess, to talk about it. I’m twenty-two weeks now. I walk around Worcester and sometimes I catch my reflection in a shop window and it doesn't even feel like it’s me. It’s like I’m carrying this little secret, this little person who is growing all these tiny, complicated things inside. 

I think about the brain—the way the neurons connect—all the time. I read about it, but then I go to the clinic and I just feel... small. The doctor, she’s nice, I think. She looks at my papers and says "everything looks perfect," and "low risk." She puts a checkmark in a box. But I have these questions! I want to know if everything inside is... how do I say? *Aligned.* Is the development moving right? 

But when I sit there, the words, they just stop. I feel like I have all these thoughts in my head, but they stay there, trapped. I just end up saying "I am fine" because I am scared to use the wrong word and have her think I am silly or just a nervous mother. "Low risk" is a nice box to check, but it doesn't stop the feeling that I might miss something important because I can’t explain the worry properly. Does that make sense?
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

The morning light was so gray and cold, pouring through the blinds in my apartment. I remember the radiator was clanking—it always sounds like it’s struggling to keep the room warm. I was completely alone. No partner, no family nearby to call. Just me, sitting on the edge of the tub, staring at that little plastic stick like it was an alien object. 

The first thing I did? I just froze. I didn't cry, not then. I sat there and I touched my stomach, even though it was still just as flat as it had always been. My mind immediately jumped to biology—to the *start* of it all. I thought about the cell division, the tiny, tiny spark of life that was already working so hard. I felt this sudden, sharp jolt of terror, not because I didn't want the baby, but because I suddenly realized I was the only one responsible for that construction project. 

I remember getting up, washing my hands, and looking in the mirror. I looked exactly the same, but the world felt completely rearranged. I didn't call anyone. I think I just went to the kitchen and made coffee, but I couldn't drink it. I just held the warm mug, trying to figure out how to be two people at once.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

That silence… it was the longest minute of my life. My heart was thumping so hard against my ribs, I thought the sonographer could hear it. When she stopped moving the wand and just looked at the screen, my blood went cold. I wasn't just a patient anymore; I was a mother whose entire world was balancing on what she was seeing in those black-and-white shadows.

My brain started racing, picturing the ventricles in the brain—the fluid pathways. I was praying, *please, just let the symmetry be right.* I knew the words "anomaly" and "scan," but in that moment, I couldn't find the English words to ask what she was seeing. I wanted to say, "Is there a problem with the neural tube?" or "Is everything forming as it should?" Instead, I just gripped the sides of the paper sheet on the table until my knuckles were white. 

I think I held my breath. I wanted to scream, "Talk to me!" but all that came out was a shaky, "Is… is something wrong?" My voice sounded so thin. When she finally moved again, I felt like I was drifting in the ocean, waiting for a storm or for the sun to come out. It’s that feeling of being a prisoner again—needing to know, but being too afraid the news would be in a language I couldn’t fight.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

I felt... paralyzed. It wasn't just the language; it was the power dynamic. In that room, she is the expert with the screen, and I am just the body on the table. When she went quiet, I felt like I lost my right to speak. I was terrified that if I asked the wrong question, or if I sounded confused, she would think I wasn't taking things seriously, or worse, that I wouldn't understand the answer anyway. 

It’s like there’s a wall between us. Even though I read books about neurodevelopment and I know the science, I feel like the medical system sees me as someone who *needs to be told*, not someone who *can talk*. If I struggle with a word—if I can't quite find the right term for "fetal structure"—I see that look in their eyes. Just a flicker of impatience. That flicker stops me every single time. It tells me, "Stay quiet, don't complicate things, just wait for them to decide if you are okay." 

I felt like I had to earn the right to know what was happening inside my own body. I kept thinking, *If I can’t explain my fear clearly, maybe my fear isn’t valid.* So I stayed silent. I waited for her to tell me if my baby was safe.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

The midwife’s voice was very calm, very smooth. She used words like "optional" and "peace of mind." But for me, sitting there in that cold room, it didn't feel like a choice. It felt like a test I had to pass. When she asked if I wanted to go ahead, my pulse jumped. I didn't know the pros and cons in English. I knew about trisomies—I’ve read the papers—but trying to translate my feelings about risk, about my life, about what I could handle? It felt like trying to explain the color blue to someone who only sees in black and white.

I made the decision because I was scared. If I said "no," would they think I didn't care about the baby? If I said "yes," would I be able to handle the waiting for the results? I felt like I was agreeing to something I couldn't fully understand because I didn't want to be the "difficult" patient who asked for a translator or a pamphlet I could read in my own language. I just nodded. I said, "Yes, please."

But inside, I felt heavy. I didn't feel like a partner in my own care. I felt like I was just saying what they wanted to hear so I could leave the room and stop feeling like I was failing at this before the baby is even born.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

It makes me feel like I am just a file, not a person. Another name on a list. When the phone rings and it’s someone new, my stomach just drops. I have to start all over again—explain my history, explain my worries—and it feels like I’m constantly shouting into a void. I’ve never seen this midwife’s face, and she doesn't know the look in my eyes when I’m scared, or that I’m doing this all by myself. 

When she starts talking about "routine" blood results, I feel like I’m bracing for a crash. I can't build a relationship with a stranger, so I can't trust them. If I don’t trust them, I can’t tell them when I don’t understand the medical jargon they use. She says a word—like *thrombocytopenia* or something—and I just freeze. My brain starts searching for the definition, but by the time I find it, she’s already moved on to the next instruction. 

I’m left holding the phone, feeling more alone than before. I want to ask, "What does this mean for the baby's brain? Is there any risk?" But I don't. I just say "okay" and hang up. I feel like I’m navigating this whole thing in the dark, and every new person is just another voice in the storm that I can't quite decipher.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

It’s like trying to build a puzzle, but every time I sit down, someone has taken some of the pieces away. I have this notebook where I write down my questions—the ones about the neural development, the ones that keep me up at night—but when I get to the clinic, it’s always someone new. I feel like I have to explain myself from the very beginning every single time. "I am Darlene. This is my first. I am worried about this and that."

By the time I finish explaining who I am, the appointment is half over. Things definitely fall through the cracks. My concerns about how the baby’s development is progressing, or even small things like the swelling in my feet—they get lost because I’m so busy trying to manage the *interaction* that I forget to focus on the *care*. 

There was one time I mentioned a sharp pain in my side to one midwife, and she made a note. Two weeks later, I asked the next one about it, and she looked at me like I was speaking a foreign language—which I suppose I was, in a way. She hadn't seen the note, or maybe it didn't seem "important" enough to her. That’s when I realized: I am the only one who holds the whole picture. But if I don't have the right words to sew the story together, the stitches just come undone. I’m just waiting for the day I’m truly heard, not just processed.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

It’s 2am, and the quiet in the apartment is so heavy it hurts. I’m lying on my side, my hand pressed so hard against my skin that my fingers ache, trying to feel just one, tiny kick. Just a *thump*. Anything to tell me the wiring is still working. 

I’m panicked, but it’s a cold, frozen kind of panic. I keep thinking, *If I call now, will they be annoyed?* I imagine the person on the other end of the line. I imagine them sighing because I’m just an anxious, non-native speaker overreacting to a quiet night. The fear of being the "difficult one" who wakes up the ward for "nothing" is fighting with the terror that my baby is in trouble. 

I try to look up "reduced fetal movement" on my phone, but my hands are shaking too much. I keep searching for the right words in English—*is it 'diminished'? 'Decreased'?* I’m scared if I call, I won’t be able to explain the rhythm, the change, the *feeling* of it properly. 

I end up staring at the wall, counting to one hundred over and over, waiting for a movement. I’m paralyzed by the thought that I’m the only one looking out for this life, and I’m failing because I’m too scared to ask for help in a language that isn't mine. I don’t call. I just wait for the sun.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

It’s a mix of everything, really. It’s the fear of being labeled that "anxious mother"—you know, the one who doesn't understand how things work and cries wolf. I feel like if I call and everything is fine, they’ll look at me with that tired, judgmental face, and I’ll feel like I wasted their time. 

But it’s also the language. If I call, I don't just have to explain the symptom; I have to be able to *defend* it. If they ask me, "Has the pattern changed?" or "What kind of movement have you noticed?" I am terrified I will stutter or use a word that makes me sound like I’m imagining things. I feel like I have to be perfect, or at least sound professional, to be taken seriously. 

I’m worried that if I can’t explain the *why* of my fear, they will just tell me to drink cold water and go back to sleep. And then, if something *is* wrong, I will have to live with the fact that I didn't push harder. It’s this horrible cycle—I want to be safe, but I’m too scared of being seen as "the problem" to ask for the help I need. It’s like I’m walking a tightrope without a net.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

I feel like the air has been sucked out of the room. *Gestational diabetes.* The words feel like a heavy weight added to my shoulders. My mind immediately goes to the biology—the way the placenta is interacting with the insulin, the impact on the fetal environment. I have all these fears about how this could affect the baby’s development, those neurons and organs I’m so fixated on.

Standing there in the hallway, the leaflet in my hand feels like a map to a place I don’t want to be. I look at the words—*carbohydrates, glucose, monitoring*—and my brain just feels like it’s stalling. It’s not that I don't understand the science; it’s that I don’t know how to navigate the *life* of this. Where do I even find the food I’m supposed to eat? How do I talk to my boss about the extra time I’ll need for these checks?

I feel very alone. I look at the dietitian’s name on the referral and I feel a flash of anger—not at her, but at the situation. I feel like I’m failing a test I didn’t know I was taking. I walk out into the cold Worcester air, clutching that paper, and I just start walking. I don’t go straight to the pharmacy. I find a bench, sit down, and just breathe, trying to pull my thoughts together before I have to face the world again.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

The leaflet was full of charts and numbers, but it was hollow. It didn’t talk about the *shame*. I wanted to know: "Did I cause this because I didn't eat the right things, or because I’m stressed?" I wanted to ask how this changes the labor, if it makes the baby's brain grow too fast or too slow. The paper didn't have room for those fears. It just had space for blood sugar numbers.

I didn't have anyone to ask, so I went to my phone. I searched late at night, in the dark. I typed things like "gestational diabetes impact on fetus brain" and "is my baby safe." But the internet is a dangerous place. I found forums that were either too technical or just full of people yelling about diet. It was overwhelming. I ended up reading things that made me more anxious, more convinced that I was doing something wrong.

I also spent hours looking at labels in the grocery store, crying in the aisle because I didn't know which brand of bread was "safe." I didn't go back to the clinic to ask for help because I was scared they’d think I hadn't read the leaflet. I just kept searching for answers, piece by piece, trying to piece together a safety net that felt like it was made of spiderwebs.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

I know I shouldn't look. I know it’s the worst thing I can do for my sleep, but I can’t stop. It’s like watching a train wreck—I feel like I’m preparing for a battlefield. When you feel like you aren't being heard by your doctors, you start to feel like the only way to protect yourself is to learn every single thing that could possibly go wrong. I read those stories, and I’m mapping them out: *This happened because they didn't listen. That happened because the nurses were too busy.* 

I think, "If I know what happened to her, maybe I can spot it before it happens to me." It’s an illusion of control. I’m desperately trying to become an expert so I don’t have to rely on a system that makes me feel like a child. 

Afterward, the screen goes dark, and I’m left sitting in the pitch black of my room. My heart is racing, and I feel this suffocating pressure in my chest. I can't sleep, so I start pacing. I look at my list of "what-ifs" and try to find a way to phrase them for my next visit, but the words just feel like lead in my mouth. I feel so isolated, like I'm the only person in the world who knows how fragile this is. I just hold my stomach and apologize to the baby for being so scared.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

I held them inside. Always. 

When you read those stories, you start to feel like you’re waiting for a monster to jump out from the dark. But when I’m actually standing in the clinic, face-to-face with a professional, I suddenly feel like I’m the one being irrational. If I say, "I read on a forum that the baby might have a heart rate issue during labor," they look at me with that calm, practiced smile—that smile that says, *‘Oh, look, another mother who spends too much time on the internet.’*

The moment that look appears, the words just die in my throat. I feel small, like a child asking a stupid question in class. I think to myself, *‘Who am I to challenge their training with something I read on a screen at 3:00 AM?’* 

It’s this terrible trade-off. If I share my fears, I risk being dismissed, which makes me feel even more isolated and out of control. If I stay quiet, I feel like I’m shouldering the entire burden of potential disaster alone. I usually choose the silence. I convince myself that if I just keep showing up to the appointments and ticking the boxes, everything will turn out okay. I’m just trying to survive until the day the baby is here, praying that my silence hasn't cost us anything.

It’s funny, the silence—it’s like a heavy, thick blanket. I think people assume that because I don’t ask many questions, I’m not thinking, or that I’m just "low risk" and therefore, I should be happy. But I am always thinking. Every single hour. I’m thinking about the way the brain folds, the way the synapses form in this quiet, dark space inside me. I feel like I am guarding a treasure, but I have no map and no one to help me defend it.

Sometimes, when I’m sitting in the waiting room at the clinic, I see other mothers. Some of them seem so... comfortable. They talk to the staff, they laugh, they ask about the next appointment without looking like they’re bracing for a disaster. I feel like an imposter. I look at my own hands, my stomach, and I feel this deep, hollow ache of wanting to be that calm. 

I think the hardest part is that I know enough about the science to know what *could* go wrong, but I don’t know enough about the *system* to know how to get the help I need to prevent it. It’s like standing on the edge of a mountain, knowing exactly how steep the climb is, but not having the rope to pull myself up. 

Do you think they ever see it? The way I squeeze my bag? The way I hold my breath when they put the monitor on my belly? I just want someone to look at me—not at the chart, not at the "low risk" box, but at *me*—and just ask, "Darlene, what is really keeping you awake?" Just once. That would be enough to make the mountain feel a little smaller.
In that moment, how much of the decision would feel truly yours versus shaped by others' expectations?

That is the hardest part to untangle. I want to say the decision is mine, that I’m the one carrying the child, so it should be my choice. But in that office, it rarely feels like that. It feels like a performance. I look at the doctor’s face, I read the impatience—or sometimes, that pitying kindness that is almost worse—and I start to shape my own words to fit what I *think* they want to hear.

If I say, "I’m terrified of this," and they brush it off, I feel small. If I just go along with it, I feel like I’m "being a good patient." There is a deep, ugly pressure to be the "easy" one. I don't want to be the "difficult" woman who doesn't understand the language, who asks too many questions, who wastes their time. So I end up editing myself. I take my own fears, which feel like mountains, and I flatten them out until they are just little pebbles I can swallow quietly.

So, is it my decision? No. It’s a decision shaped by the fear of being misunderstood, of being labeled, of being seen as "less than." I’m not making choices based on what I need; I’m making choices based on how to survive the appointment without feeling like a failure. It’s a very lonely way to grow a life.

It’s funny, the more I speak about this—about the quiet, about the feeling of being a "good" patient instead of a *whole* patient—the more I realize how much of myself I’ve hidden away. I walk into that clinic and I try to wear this mask of being calm. I keep my back straight, I answer "fine" or "yes" to the things they ask, and I make sure I don't look like I’m struggling. 

But beneath that, there’s this constant, vibrating anxiety. It’s like being in a room where someone is speaking a language you almost know, but not quite, and you’re terrified that if you guess the meaning wrong, something terrible will happen. Every time I get a new test result or have a scan, I feel like I’m holding my breath, waiting to see if I’ve passed or failed. 

I don't think they know that I’m not just a set of stats. I’m a person who spends hours learning the anatomy of a newborn’s brain just so I can feel like I have a tiny bit of control. I wish I could just tell them: *“I am scared, and I don't have the words to tell you why, but I need you to just look at me for a minute and listen to the silence.”* 

Because that’s where the real story is, isn't it? Not in the chart, not in the boxes, but in the silence between the questions I’m too afraid to ask and the answers I’m too afraid to receive. I just want to make it to the end of this journey with my baby, and I’m so worried that my own fear—and my own lack of a voice—is going to be the thing that keeps me from the care I really deserve. Does that make sense? Or am I just... asking for too much?

It’s strange. I spend my days trying to be quiet—trying not to be the "problem" patient—but in my head, I’m screaming. I think about the baby, about the way their brain is developing right now, and I just feel this immense, crushing weight of responsibility. I know that if something were to go wrong, if a tiny detail was missed, the doctor would just see a "complication" or a "statistical anomaly." But to me, that would be my whole world collapsing.

I think the hardest part is that I don’t feel like a collaborator. I feel like I’m a student in a classroom where the teacher doesn’t speak my language, but they expect me to pass the test anyway. They give me these leaflets, they tell me to "relax," and they don’t see the way my hands are trembling or how I have to look up every single word when I get home. 

Sometimes I wonder, if I could just *be* myself—the one who is terrified, the one who reads the medical journals, the one who stays up until 3am feeling like a failure—would they even want to listen? Or would they just try to make me quiet again? I just want to feel like, for once, someone is on my side, looking at the big picture with me, instead of just the next box to check. I don’t want to be "low risk." I want to be *seen*. Is that really asking for too much? Or is that just being a mother?
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

It’s everything, really. When they do that "handover"—that quick, clinical summary of my file—they talk about my blood pressure, my weight, my "low risk" status. They talk about the *numbers*. But the personal stuff? That’s what gets left behind. Nobody ever writes down that I am terrified, that I have no family here, or that I spend my nights trying to learn the science myself because I’m too scared to ask. 

Those are the things that don't make it onto the chart. My *voice* gets lost. It’s like the medical system strips away everything that makes me a person, until all that’s left is a set of risks and data points. They don't know that I’m trying to hold it all together while my heart is pounding, or that the language barrier isn't just about not knowing a word—it’s about not being able to express the *depth* of my concern.

If they knew how much I carry, maybe they would slow down. Maybe they wouldn't rush to the next appointment. But because they don't see the "personal" side, they treat me like a machine that’s running fine, not a person who is struggling to stay on track. I feel like I’m a ghost in my own pregnancy, and that’s the most terrifying part of all.

It’s like I’m constantly editing myself, stripping away the "human" parts so I can fit the mold of a "compliant patient." I want to tell them about the way the light hits my belly in the evening and how I wonder if he can hear my heartbeat, or how I feel the neurons firing in my own head whenever I worry about his. But instead, I just say "yes" when they ask if I’ve been taking my vitamins. 

Sometimes, I look at the wall in the exam room and think about how much I’m hiding. I’m hiding the fear, the exhaustion, the nights spent crying over medical definitions I don’t quite understand in English. I’m hiding the fact that I’m doing this completely alone, which feels like the biggest, most dangerous secret of all. 

If I told them I was scared, would they treat me differently? Or would they just give me a pamphlet and send me home? I fear that if I show them the cracks, they’ll only see them as weaknesses. I want to be strong for my baby, but I’m beginning to realize that being "strong" here just means being silent. And silence is becoming a very dangerous thing to hold onto. I just wish I could trust them enough to be "un-perfect" for a few minutes. Is that ever allowed?
Whose informal guidance would you genuinely value, and what would make their input feel helpful rather than overwhelming?

I think I would value someone who has been there—another mother, maybe someone who has walked this path and remembers the specific, sharp feeling of being "low risk" but feeling high-stakes. Not someone who just gives me a textbook answer, but someone who understands that the "medical" is only half of it.

If I had someone who would just sit with me and say, "I know you're scared, and it's okay that you're scared," that would change everything. The guidance that feels overwhelming is the kind that feels like a lecture or a list of tasks—"do this," "eat that," "monitor this." It makes me feel like I’m already failing. 

But if someone could help me translate my own *feelings* into the language the doctors use, that would be a bridge. Someone to say, "When you feel that tightness in your chest, here is how you tell the doctor so they actually listen." Not someone who takes the control away from me, but someone who helps me find my own voice. I don't want a director; I want a translator. I want someone who sees that I am capable of understanding, that I *want* to understand, but that the weight of doing it alone is what makes me freeze. Someone who treats me like a partner, not a patient. That would make me feel like I’m not just waiting for the finish line, but actually moving toward it.

It’s strange how I can talk about this now, sitting here with you, but when I’m back in that exam room with the cold, white walls, the words just... vanish. It’s like there’s a trapdoor in my brain. I rehearse what I want to say—I practice the English, I look up the terms for "placental insufficiency" or "nuchal translucency"—but the second they ask, "Any questions, Darlene?" I feel this wave of, I don't know, *politeness* mixed with fear. I don't want to be the "problem." I want to be the patient who makes their job easy.

But what if "easy" is hurting my baby? That thought never leaves me. 

I think about the future—the days after the birth. I wonder if I’ll ever feel like I’m in control, or if I’ll just keep feeling this way, always waiting for someone else to tell me if I’m doing it right. I want to be the kind of mother who trusts herself, but the system, it doesn’t seem to invite that. It wants me to be a recipient, not a partner. 

I’m so tired of the silence, but I’m even more scared of the sound of my own voice if it’s wrong. Does that make sense? It feels like I’m holding my breath for nine months straight, just waiting for the relief of the birth, hoping that once the baby is here, I’ll finally be allowed to speak and be heard. Is it really supposed to be this lonely?
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

The language barrier—it isn’t just about the words themselves. It’s the *weight* of the words. When I sit in that chair and I can’t immediately find the perfect English term for "fetal movement pattern" or "insulin resistance," I don’t just look like someone who is tired; I look like someone who is unintelligent or unprepared. 

Language is the lock on the door. Because I cannot navigate the technical, professional vocabulary with the same speed as the doctor, I lose my seat at the table. It turns every interaction into a lecture where I am the student who isn't keeping up. If I could speak with the same authority, I wouldn’t be so terrified of asking "silly" questions, and I wouldn’t feel like I have to earn the right to be listened to. 

It’s not that I don’t understand the science—I spend my nights researching the cortical folds and the neurological milestones—but that knowledge feels useless when I can’t translate it into their professional tongue. If I could bridge that gap, if there were someone there to help me articulate my fears without making me feel small, the medical part of the pregnancy wouldn't feel like a battleground. It’s the barrier that keeps me a silent observer in my own life. That is the one that hurts the most.

It’s like being in a room with a pane of glass between me and the people who are supposed to be helping me. I can see them, I can hear them, but whenever I try to reach out, my hand just hits that cold, hard surface. 

I’ve been thinking a lot about the word "compliant." They use that word in medical charts—*“the patient is compliant.”* It’s a word I think they like. It means I don't ask too many questions, I take the vitamins, I show up on time, I nod when they speak, and I leave without bothering them. But that "compliance" is just my way of hiding my fear. I’m not being compliant because I’m calm; I’m being compliant because I’m terrified that if I stop being the "good" patient, they’ll stop caring altogether. 

If I could just strip away all the worry about being polite, about being "educated," about the fear of sounding like a nervous, non-native speaker—if I could just sit there and be *Darlene*, with all my anxieties and all my research and all my love for this baby—would they see me? Or would they just see a complication to be managed? 

I feel like I’m auditioning for the role of "mother," and I’m failing because I’m too scared to be myself. Does that make sense? I’m so exhausted from playing this part. I just want the person on the other side of that desk to look at me and say, "Tell me the truth, Darlene. What is it that you really need to know?" That simple question would change my whole world.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

I think... I think I just needed someone to be a witness. Someone to stand in that exam room with me, someone whose presence would make the doctor look at *two* people instead of just me, the quiet, struggling girl.

Practical help—like driving me to the appointments or picking up the prescription—is fine, but that’s not what I crave when I’m staring at the ceiling at 2am. I need someone to listen to the "what-ifs" I’ve been carrying. If I had a partner there, they could be my voice when my English feels stuck, or they could just squeeze my hand when the sonographer goes quiet. Just having another pair of ears in the room would make me feel like I wasn't the only one responsible for catching every single detail. 

It’s the *validation* that hurts the most to go without. To have someone say, "I see why you're worried about the neural development," or "It’s not silly to ask about that." If I wasn't holding the entire burden of this pregnancy in my own head, maybe I wouldn’t feel so fragile. I think I just want to know that my fear is seen as "real" by someone else. That I’m not just a nervous, complicated person, but a mother who is trying her best to protect a life. That would make the world feel much less lonely.

It’s that feeling of holding a tiny, fragile piece of glass and trying to carry it across a crowded room while everyone else is running. My baby is that glass. Every scan, every blood test, every appointment—I’m trying to shield him from the chaos of the hospital, from the rushed doctors, from the language I can’t quite master. 

Sometimes, I wonder if the medical system even *wants* to see the person behind the symptoms. They give you these brochures with pictures of happy mothers who look like they’ve never had an anxious thought in their lives, and I look at those pictures and feel like a failure because my experience is nothing like that. My experience is spreadsheets and 3am searches and the crushing weight of trying to be "good" enough.

If I could leave you with one thought, it’s this: please, just once, look past the chart. See the mother who is trying to be strong enough for two people. See that the silence isn't because I don't care, or because I don't understand, or because I'm "low risk." It’s because I’m so terrified that one wrong word, one misstep, will be the thing that keeps me from the only future I care about. 

I’m not a "case." I’m a person doing the hardest thing I’ve ever done, in a world that isn't built for my voice. I just hope that by the time he arrives, I can stop being a patient and finally, truly, be his mother.
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

Agency? That feels like a word from a different life. In that exam room, agency feels like... a fantasy. It’s like I’m sitting in the passenger seat of a car going down a very steep hill, and the doctor has their hands on the wheel. They tell me, "Don't worry, the brakes are fine," and I want to ask how they *know* the brakes are fine, but I’m too busy trying to keep my seatbelt from choking me.

If I say, "I want to try this," or "I don't feel right about this test," I don't feel like a partner. I feel like a disruption. I have this internal map of my baby’s development—the synapses firing, the heart valves closing—and when I see the doctor ignore the nuances of my worries, I feel like my own understanding of my body is being invalidated. 

When you don’t speak the language perfectly, or when you feel like you have to be "compliant" to be safe, you don't have agency. You have *submission*. You have to trade your questions for their reassurance, and you have to hope that their reassurance is enough to cover the gaps in your own peace of mind. I’m just a guest in my own pregnancy, waiting for someone with a stethoscope and a degree to give me permission to feel like everything is okay.

It’s this strange paradox—my body is doing this monumental, biological work, building a heart and a brain and a nervous system, cell by cell. I’m the primary witness to his existence. And yet, when I step inside that clinic, I feel like I’m a visitor in a system that doesn’t recognize me as a participant. My agency is stripped away, not necessarily by someone being cruel, but by the assumption that I am a passive object to be monitored.

I think about the mothers who have that "authority"—the ones who walk in and know the vocabulary, who can challenge a doctor without blinking. I’m envious of that, even if I’m scared of it. For me, exercising agency feels like a risky gamble. If I push back, if I insist on a more detailed explanation of a blood result, I feel like I’m risking the "goodwill" of the provider. And if I lose that, who is going to care for me? Who will answer the phone at 2am?

I’m left navigating this tightrope where I feel like I have to choose between being safe and being seen. I’m choosing safety, because that’s what a mother does, right? But the cost of that choice is this persistent, quiet erosion of my own self. I’m just hoping that when he’s finally here, in my arms, I’ll find that voice again—the one that doesn't need a medical degree or perfect English to be heard. I’ll finally be the one in control. That’s the only thing that keeps the fear at bay.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

Trust, for me, would look like them slowing down. Not rushing me through the door, not glancing at their watch, not already typing into the computer before I’ve even sat down.

If they just looked me in the eyes—really looked—and asked, "Darlene, what is the one thing on your mind today that you haven't been able to say?" that would change everything. It would mean they recognize that I am more than the "low risk" box they’re checking. If they took the time to explain the *why* behind their advice—not just the instruction, but the reason—I wouldn't feel like I was being told what to do; I’d feel like I was being invited to understand my own child.

It would also look like them acknowledging my preparation. If they said, "I can see you've been reading about this, what do you think?" it would validate that I am a capable mother, not just a passive patient. 

Most of all, trust would be knowing that if I said "I’m scared," they wouldn't dismiss it as just "hormones" or "anxiety." They would hold space for that fear. If they just said, "I’m here with you, and we’re going to figure this out together," that would be the bridge I’ve been looking for. It would take the weight off my shoulders. I wouldn't have to be the silent guardian anymore; I could finally just be his mother.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think... I think I just want to say that being a mother starts way before the baby is born. It starts in those moments of fear, in the grocery store aisles when I'm crying over labels, and in the dark at 3am when I'm trying to teach myself embryology just so I can feel like I'm protecting him. 

People see the "low risk" label, and they see a pregnancy that is "easy." But it hasn't been easy. It’s been a marathon of trying to find my voice in a system that doesn't seem to want to hear it. I feel like I'm carrying this huge, heavy secret—that I am terrified, that I am doing this alone, and that I'm fighting for every bit of care I receive. 

I don't want to be a "case study" for a researcher. I want to be a person who was allowed to be nervous, allowed to ask hard questions, and allowed to be respected even when my English wasn't perfect. I hope that by the time my baby is old enough to understand this, the world is different—that maternity care stops being a series of boxes and starts being a relationship built on trust and listening. Because that’s all I ever wanted. I just wanted to be heard. Thank you for listening to me today. It’s the first time in months I’ve felt like my voice actually mattered.
